---
title: "pySEB Documentation"
subtitle: "Executable tutorials for the Scattering Equation Builder"
---

pySEB constructs small-angle-scattering expressions for composite structures
assembled from analytic, symbolic, and numerical subunits.

It combines a C++14 scattering core with Python bindings, a portable symbolic
backend, optional GiNaC support, and native SymPy conversion. Structures are
built by connecting reusable subunits at named or distributed reference
points.

## From a model to a scattering expression

![A four-step SEB workflow: choose a target structure, assemble it from subunits, derive the symbolic scattering expression, and evaluate or plot it.](https://github.com/Tobionecenobi/SEB/assets/45657039/59a2129c-ce13-4534-8aef-69e5b7e02600){fig-alt="SEB workflow from a target structure through subunit construction and symbolic derivation to a scattering plot." width="95%"}

The same structure graph supports symbolic output, direct numerical
evaluation, parameter sweeps, and contrast changes. Python and C++ use the
same `World`, `Add`, `Link`, names, tags, and reference-point concepts.

## Install pySEB

Create and activate an isolated environment on Linux or macOS:

```bash
python3 -m venv .venv-pyseb
source .venv-pyseb/bin/activate
python -m pip install --upgrade pip
python -m pip install pyseb
```

Confirm the installation:

```bash
python -c "import pyseb; print(type(pyseb.World()).__name__)"
```

The command prints `World`. Windows PowerShell instructions and source builds
are covered by the
[platform build guides](https://github.com/Tobionecenobi/pySEB/wiki/Build-Guides).

## Typical workflow

1. Create a `World`.
2. Add analytic or numerical subunits.
3. Link reference points and name the completed structure.
4. Request symbolic expressions or evaluate scattering quantities for q and a
   parameter dictionary.

```python
import pyseb

world = pyseb.World("FirstModel")
graph = world.Add("GaussianPolymer", "blockA")
world.Link("GaussianPolymer", "blockB.end1", "blockA.end2")
world.Add(graph, "diblock")

parameters = {
    "beta_blockA": 1.0,
    "beta_blockB": 0.8,
    "Rg_blockA": 5.0,
    "Rg_blockB": 8.0,
}
print(world.EvaluateFormFactor("diblock", parameters, [0.001, 0.01, 0.1]))
```

## What pySEB provides

- Analytic models for polymers, rods, circles, disks, spheres, cylinders, and
  spherical shells.
- Structures assembled through specific or distributed reference points.
- Reusable nested graphs for stars, micelles, dendrimers, and published models.
- Normalized and unnormalized form factors, amplitudes, phase factors, and
  size measures.
- Numerical Debye sphere clouds, callback-defined subunits, and PDB-derived
  atom clouds.
- Portable and GiNaC C++ backends plus SymPy conversion and code export.

## Supported subunits

![Overview of SEB analytic subunits and their specific or distributed reference points.](https://github.com/Tobionecenobi/SEB/assets/22714271/8ca00de9-60e3-4895-bc50-648d4b9f6d68){fig-alt="Solid cylinder, solid sphere, spherical shells, disk, Gaussian polymer, Gaussian loop, thin circle, thin rod, and point subunits with reference points." width="95%"}

The [Subunit Catalogue](https://github.com/Tobionecenobi/pySEB/wiki/Subunit-Catalogue)
provides the current class names, parameters, reference points, equations, and
Python/C++ examples, including numerical and PDB-derived models added after the
original overview figure.

## Start learning

::: {.grid}
::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-6}
### First model

Create a Python environment, install pySEB, and build the same Gaussian
diblock in Python and C++.

[Build a diblock](tutorials/diblock-copolymer.qmd){.btn .btn-primary}
:::

::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-6}
### Choose by task

Continue with micelles, nested structures, parameter sweeps, or a mixed
PDB–polymer model. Every page includes executable Python and compiled C++14.

[Browse tutorials](tutorials/index.qmd){.btn .btn-primary}
:::
:::

## Reference documentation

The [pySEB wiki](https://github.com/Tobionecenobi/pySEB/wiki) contains build
guides, user manuals, public API summaries, the complete subunit catalogue,
theory, and troubleshooting information.

## Python and C++

Python and C++ receive parallel coverage throughout the documentation:

- [Python user guide](https://github.com/Tobionecenobi/pySEB/wiki/Python-User-Guide)
- [C++ user guide](https://github.com/Tobionecenobi/pySEB/wiki/Cpp-User-Guide)
- [Python API reference](https://github.com/Tobionecenobi/pySEB/wiki/Python-API-Reference)
- [C++ API reference](https://github.com/Tobionecenobi/pySEB/wiki/Cpp-API-Reference)
- [Symbolic backends](https://github.com/Tobionecenobi/pySEB/wiki/Symbolic-Backends)

## Example structures

::: {.grid}
::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-6}
![Linear chains assembled from different subunit types.](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Tobionecenobi/pySEB/main/PaperFigs/fig10.png){fig-alt="Figure 10: linear chains assembled from different SEB subunit types."}
:::

::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-6}
![Dendrimers assembled recursively from reusable structures.](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Tobionecenobi/pySEB/main/PaperFigs/fig11.png){fig-alt="Figure 11: recursively assembled dendrimer structures and form factors."}
:::

::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-6}
![Rods and polymers attached to geometric surfaces.](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Tobionecenobi/pySEB/main/PaperFigs/fig12.png){fig-alt="Figure 12: rods and polymers attached to surfaces of geometric bodies."}
:::

::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-6}
![A chain of five diblock-copolymer stars and its contrast-dependent form factor.](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Tobionecenobi/pySEB/main/PaperFigs/fig13.png){fig-alt="Figure 13: chain of five diblock-copolymer stars and scattering curves for several contrasts."}
:::
:::

These published models demonstrate linear composition, recursive reuse,
distributed surface references, hierarchical nesting, and contrast variation.
The [Figure 13 tutorial](tutorials/figure13-star-chain.qmd) reconstructs the
star chain step by step in executable Python and C++.

## Source and examples

- [Source repository](https://github.com/Tobionecenobi/pySEB)
- [Python examples](https://github.com/Tobionecenobi/pySEB/tree/main/pyseb/examples/python)
- [C++ examples](https://github.com/Tobionecenobi/pySEB/tree/main/Examples)
- [Build and installation guides](https://github.com/Tobionecenobi/pySEB/wiki/Build-Guides)
- [Publications and citation](https://github.com/Tobionecenobi/pySEB/wiki/Publications-and-Citation)
- [Project status and roadmap](https://github.com/Tobionecenobi/pySEB/wiki/Project-Status-and-Roadmap)
